articles.title: One dead in Brighton Park fire
articles.description:
One person died after a fire in Brighton Park Thursday night, according to Chicago fire officials.

Firefighters responded to a call of a rubbish fire at 5040 S. Campbell Avenue at 6:41 p.m., said Chicago Fire Chief Juan Hernandez. A man, whose age wasn’t known, was found on the first floor of the building and taken to Christ Hospital where he later died.

Due to the thick smoke and heavy fire, firefighters initially had to pull back, Hernandez said. As the fire spread and damaged the homes on either side of the initial building, more than 150 firefighters were called to the scene to assist.

A police officer had tried to knock on the door when the fire was first reported, and before firefighters could arrive, but was turned away until the smoke became heavier, Hernandez said.

The cause of the fire is under investigation.

articles.title: 2 drug suspects nabbed in Quezon; P2.1-million shabu seized
articles.description: LUCENA CITY — Police arrested two alleged high-value drug suspects and seized suspected “shabu” (crystal meth) worth up to P2.1 million during a buy-bust operation in Tiaong, Quezon, on Thursday. Colonel Romulo Albacea, director of the Quezon Police Provincial Office (QPPO), said anti-illegal drug operatives arrested the suspects, identified only by their aliases “Randy,” 49, and “Gary,” 53, in Barangay Lalig at around 6:25 a.m. The suspects allegedly sold P1,000 worth of shabu to an undercover police operative during the operation. READ: Packs of suspected shabu found in Quezon island town coastline Authorities confiscated one knot-tied plastic bag and three [...]... articles.title: Cities are taking a surprising step to avoid feeding info to Trump's ICE: report
articles.description: Cities contracting with Flock Safety to run networks of license plate reader cameras are growing increasingly alarmed about the company's involvement with the Trump administration's mass deportation schemes, to the point that some are now covering their own cameras in trash bags, a new report revealed. According to 404 Media, one city bagging up its cameras is Dayton, Ohio, which followed "months of resident outrage, a scandal in which the city was sharing Flock camera data for immigration enforcement, apparently by accident, and a $30,000 audit into how the cameras are being used." They had to bag the cameras rather than take them down outright because "police there are unsure whether the cameras are still active, and the city also doesn’t seem to know whether it is allowed to take the cameras down." This follows a similar move by the city of Evanston, Illinois, which, following media reports that "data from the cameras was making its way to Immigration and Customs Enforcement through Flock’s national camera network," bagged up its cameras until Flock could uninstall them, said the report. These moves, 404 Media continued, show cities "feel that they do not have the ability to unilaterally decide when to stop using Flock surveillance cameras" under the terms of the contracts they signed. The American Civil Liberties Union has sounded the alarm on Flock, which they claim is building up the infrastructure for a "mass surveillance state" with its network of license plate readers that were sold to cities as a tool for public safety. Meanwhile, immigration enforcement is not the only politicized use of the Flock camera network. Last year, it was reported that a police officer in Johnson County, Texas, used a national Flock plate database to search for a woman who had an abortion .
